That’s why I try to limit my time as much as possible. When I do engage, I look for accounts promoting physical wellness rather than quick fixes for everything. For example, during quarantine periods when people were stuck at home due to COVID-19 restrictions in Italy, many posts claimed miracle workouts without proper scientific backing.\n\nI believe the best way to manage fake content is by relying on trusted sources and filtering algorithms. My high agreeableness means I value community guidance over individual opinions online. Social media platforms like Instagram should incorporate more robust AI systems that flag unverified claims about nutrition or exercise routines. Combining this with my own personal experience—training people in person—I can better identify what works versus hype.\n\nAnother approach is to cultivate a critical mindset, especially regarding health topics which often have misconceptions spread virulently. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
As a fitness trainer in Bari who spends just one hour daily on social media, I've found that dealing with fake content requires patience and a clear focus. It's easy to get caught up in the endless stream of misinformation or overly sensational posts, but my depression makes me more vulnerable to negativity online. That’s why I try to limit my time as much as possible. When I do engage, I look for accounts promoting physical wellness rather than quick fixes for everything. For example, during quarantine periods when people were stuck at home due to COVID-19 restrictions in Italy, many posts claimed miracle workouts without proper scientific backing.

I believe the best way to manage fake content is by relying on trusted sources and filtering algorithms. My high agreeableness means I value community guidance over individual opinions online. Social media platforms like Instagram should incorporate more robust AI systems that flag unverified claims about nutrition or exercise routines. Combining this with my own personal experience—training people in person—I can better identify what works versus hype.

Another approach is to cultivate a critical mindset, especially regarding health topics which often have misconceptions spread virulently. Since I'm not neurotic and generally low-key online presence despite being somewhat open-minded personally, fake content doesn't overwhelm me too much. But for those struggling with mental health or addiction issues through social media exposure, stricter moderation policies are essential.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
I am a fitness trainer dealing with limited screen time on social media due to personal depression and my native Italian background emphasizes community values over sensationalism. I manage fake content by focusing on reliable sources within the physical wellness domain and using basic filtering techniques.

My high agreeableness trait makes me prioritize group wisdom when evaluating health claims online, whereas moderate conscientiousness helps me stick to evidence-based practices rather than quick-fix trends.
